title: Former Socceroos Captain Wade Diagnosed With Probable CTE
dek: Former Australia captain Paul Wade, 64, has been diagnosed with probable chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative brain disease linked to repeated head impacts. Wade captained the Socceroos between 1990 and 1996 and earned 84 caps. His diagnosis renews focus on youth heading restrictions and player safety in soccer.
author: The GoCheckMySports Desk
key_fact: Paul Wade, former Socceroos captain, diagnosed with probable CTE following neurological assessment in late 2025.
bottom_line: Wade's diagnosis is probable, not definitive, and will only be confirmed post-mortem, leaving a medical gray zone. Watch whether Dr. Mobbs' heading restriction recommendation gains traction with Australian football's governing bodies and whether other former players come forward with similar diagnoses.
body: Paul Wade, former captain of the Australian national soccer team, has been diagnosed with probable chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), a progressive neurodegenerative brain disease associated with repeated head impacts and concussions. Wade, now 64, received the diagnosis following a neurological assessment and specialist evaluation in late 2025, according to ESPN reporting from July 23. He initially presented with suspected CTE in late 2024 after reporting neurological symptoms.

Wade captained the Socceroos between 1990 and 1996 and earned 84 caps for Australia, making his international debut in 1986. During his domestic career, he played 345 games in the National Soccer League for Croydon, Green Gully, Brunswick, and South Melbourne. His symptoms, reported to ESPN, include memory loss, increased short-temperedness, and mood swings. According to ESPN, Wade's neurologist told him he will likely die because of CTE. CTE can only be definitively diagnosed after death, making Wade's diagnosis probable rather than confirmed.

Dr Rowena Mobbs, Wade's neurologist and one of Australia's leading concussion and CTE experts, has recommended a reduction in heading at the junior level, including removing all heading under age 12. The Professional Footballers Australia produced a mini documentary in which Wade reflects on his career and confronts the realities of living with a neurodegenerative condition linked to repeated head trauma. PFA chief executive Beau Busch stated, per ESPN, that "Australian football must proactively pursue being Australia's safest sport."

Wade's case joins a growing list of high-profile athletes in contact sports diagnosed with CTE or probable CTE. Rugby League legend Wally Lewis has been diagnosed with probable CTE. In Australian Rules Football, Polly Farmer, who died in 2019 at age 84, was the first former AFL/VFL player diagnosed with definitive CTE post-mortem. Former St Kilda captain Danny Frawley and former Richmond player Shane Tuck were also diagnosed with CTE, per ESPN's reporting.

Wade emphasized to ESPN that he does not want to discourage anyone from playing football, citing the many positive health benefits of the sport. The tension between his diagnosis and that statement underscores the dilemma facing governing bodies: acknowledging a documented risk while maintaining the game's participation base.
